statements","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Working with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

This closes #5352 
This closes #3113 This closes #5352

with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

This closes #5352 
This closes #3113 statements","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Working with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

This closes #5352 
This closes #3113 {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","id":2713710215,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6hv-6H","number":5549,"title":"IMPLICIT declaration_type_spec","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-12-03T00:51:25Z","updated_at":"2024-12-03T00:51:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently, the `parser.yy` grammar uses local rules for imitating the _kind-selector_ component of an (intrinsic) _declaration-type-spec_.  While these rules handle some simple cases correctly (e.g. `implicit real(8) (a)`), it is easy to generate examples that should be accepted, but are not:
```f90
implicit real(kind=8) (a)
implicit real(4+4) (b)
implicit character(len=20, kind=c_char) (c)
```

Furthering the problem are inputs that are accepted using the `"(" letter_spec_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")"` style of rules, which are never checked or resolved as _scalar-int-constant-expr_:
```f90
program bad_kind
  use constants
  implicit real(r8k) (a)
  implicit real(dp) (b)
  implicit real(q-l) (c)
  print *, kind(a), kind(b), kind(c)
end program bad_kind
```
prints `4    4    4`, without even attempting to lookup values for `r8k` or `dp` or compute the value `q-l`.  Note that these problems are exhibited both before (SHA c75347178f1982e6) and after (SHA aed88c2ec20c7e4591) my commit to add _implicit-spec-list_ 

The appropriate solution to these problems is to change the grammar to simply use the `declaration_type_spec` rule, which handles all of these cases.  That would look like:
```bison
implicit_statement
    : KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE sep { $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE(TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE "(" implicit_none_spec_star ")" sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE2($4, TRIVIA_AFTER($6, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT($2, TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    ;

implicit_spec_list
    : implicit_spec_list "," implicit_spec { $$ = $1; LIST_ADD($$, $3); }
    | implicit_spec { LIST_NEW($$); LIST_ADD($$, $1); }
    ;

implicit_spec
    : declaration_type_spec "(" letter_spec_list ")" {
           $$ = IMPLICIT_SPEC($1, $3, @$); }
    ;
```
The complete implementation of this change can be seen in the draft PR linked below.

The reason that this is draft is that it appears to introduce five *essential* shift/reduce conflicts (see #5426), that don't go away in a standard LALR(1) grammar with reserved keywords.  I believe that this stems from the fact that the `letter_spec` rule, as implemented in LFortran, overlaps completely with the _kind-selector_ _scalar-int-constant-expr_:   letter-spec-lists like `(a)` and `(a-b)` can be parsed either as a letter-spec-list or as an expr. 

With the above grammar, Bison finds a shift/reduce conflict for the INTEGER, REAL, COMPLEX, and LOGICAL intrinsic types that take a _kind-selector_, and the CHARACTER type that takes a _char-selector_.  It eventually resolves it correctly by taking the shift option, but *I* don't know how to express the grammar in a way that prevents this ambiguity.

Using a newer version of Bison, we can get it to produce counterexamples that show the shift/reduce conflict.  This is the output for that diagnostic for a REAL declaration type spec:
```
State 480

  369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •  [TK_NAME, "/", ",", "(", ")", "::", KW_ABSTRACT, ..., KW_WHILE, KW_WRITE]
  370                    | KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
  371                    | KW_REAL • "*" TK_INTEGER

    "*"  shift, and go to state 505
    "("  shift, and go to state 506

    "("       [reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)]
    $default  reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)

    shift/reduce conflict on token "(":
      369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •
      370 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      First example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Shift derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                                            END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: implicit_statement
                  ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                                   sep
                                     ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                            ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec                         "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                   ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                          ↳ 370: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      Second example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Reduce derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                               END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: script_unit
                  ↳ 12: implicit_statement
                        ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                sep
                                           ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                                  ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec      "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                         ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                                ↳ 369: KW_REAL •
```
Details of this report can be found at: https://www.gnu.org/software/bison/manual/bison.html#Counterexamples

The PR referenced below compiles and works by extending the number of shift/reduce conflicts.  I'm not sure how to proceed: all suggestions are welcome!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","id":2713710215,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6hv-6H","number":5549,"title":"IMPLICIT declaration_type_spec","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-12-03T00:51:25Z","updated_at":"2024-12-03T17:38:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently, the `parser.yy` grammar uses rules local to `implicit_spec` for imitating the _kind-selector_ component of an (intrinsic) _declaration-type-spec_ in an IMPLICIT statement.  While these rules handle some simple cases correctly (e.g. `implicit real(8) (a)`), it is easy to generate examples that should be accepted, but are not:
```f90
implicit real(kind=8) (a)
implicit real(4+4) (b)
implicit character(len=20, kind=c_char) (c)
```

Furthering the problem are inputs that are accepted using the `"(" letter_spec_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")"` style of rules, which are never checked or resolved as _scalar-int-constant-expr_:
```f90
program bad_kind
  use constants
  implicit real(r8k) (a)
  implicit real(dp) (b)
  implicit real(q-l) (c)
  print *, kind(a), kind(b), kind(c)
end program bad_kind
```
prints `4    4    4`, without even attempting to lookup values for `r8k` or `dp` or compute the value `q-l`.  Note that these problems are exhibited both before (SHA c75347178f1982e6) and after (SHA aed88c2ec20c7e4591) my commit to add _implicit-spec-list_ 

The appropriate solution to these problems is to change the grammar to simply use the `declaration_type_spec` rule, which handles all of these cases.  That would look like:
```bison
implicit_statement
    : KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE sep { $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE(TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE "(" implicit_none_spec_star ")" sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE2($4, TRIVIA_AFTER($6, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT($2, TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    ;

implicit_spec_list
    : implicit_spec_list "," implicit_spec { $$ = $1; LIST_ADD($$, $3); }
    | implicit_spec { LIST_NEW($$); LIST_ADD($$, $1); }
    ;

implicit_spec
    : declaration_type_spec "(" letter_spec_list ")" {
           $$ = IMPLICIT_SPEC($1, $3, @$); }
    ;
```
The complete implementation of this change can be seen in the draft PR linked below.

The reason that this is draft is that it appears to introduce five *essential* shift/reduce conflicts (see #5426), that don't go away in a standard LALR(1) grammar with reserved keywords.  I believe that this stems from the fact that the `letter_spec` rule, as implemented in LFortran, overlaps completely with the _kind-selector_ _scalar-int-constant-expr_:   letter-spec-lists like `(a)` and `(a-b)` can be parsed either as a letter-spec-list or as an expr. 

With the above grammar, Bison finds a shift/reduce conflict for the INTEGER, REAL, COMPLEX, and LOGICAL intrinsic types that take a _kind-selector_, and the CHARACTER type that takes a _char-selector_.  It eventually resolves it correctly by taking the shift option, but *I* don't know how to express the grammar in a way that prevents this ambiguity.

Using a newer version of Bison, we can get it to produce counterexamples that show the shift/reduce conflict.  This is the output for that diagnostic for a REAL declaration type spec:
```
State 480

  369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •  [TK_NAME, "/", ",", "(", ")", "::", KW_ABSTRACT, ..., KW_WHILE, KW_WRITE]
  370                    | KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
  371                    | KW_REAL • "*" TK_INTEGER

    "*"  shift, and go to state 505
    "("  shift, and go to state 506

    "("       [reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)]
    $default  reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)

    shift/reduce conflict on token "(":
      369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •
      370 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      First example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Shift derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                                            END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: implicit_statement
                  ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                                   sep
                                     ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                            ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec                         "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                   ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                          ↳ 370: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      Second example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Reduce derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                               END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: script_unit
                  ↳ 12: implicit_statement
                        ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                sep
                                           ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                                  ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec      "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                         ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                                ↳ 369: KW_REAL •
```
Details of this report can be found at: https://www.gnu.org/software/bison/manual/bison.html#Counterexamples

The PR referenced below compiles and works by extending the number of shift/reduce conflicts.   implicit real(r8k) (a)
  implicit real(dp) (b)
  implicit real(q-l) (c)
  print *, kind(a), kind(b), kind(c)
end program bad_kind
```
prints `4    4    4`, without even attempting to lookup values for `r8k` or `dp` or compute the value `q-l`.  Note that these problems are exhibited both before (SHA c75347178f1982e6) and after (SHA aed88c2ec20c7e4591) my commit to add _implicit-spec-list_ 

The appropriate solution to these problems is to change the grammar to simply use the `declaration_type_spec` rule, which handles all of these cases.  That would look like:
```bison
implicit_statement
    : KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE sep { $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE(TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE "(" implicit_none_spec_star ")" sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE2($4, TRIVIA_AFTER($6, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT($2, TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    ;

implicit_spec_list
    : implicit_spec_list "," implicit_spec { $$ = $1; LIST_ADD($$, $3); }
    | implicit_spec { LIST_NEW($$); LIST_ADD($$, $1); }
    ;

implicit_spec
    : declaration_type_spec "(" letter_spec_list ")" {
           $$ = IMPLICIT_SPEC($1, $3, @$); }
    ;
```
The complete implementation of this change can be seen in the draft PR linked below.

The reason that this is draft is that it appears to introduce five *essential* shift/reduce conflicts (see #5426), that don't go away in a standard LALR(1) grammar with reserved keywords.  I believe that this stems from the fact that the `letter_spec` rule, as implemented in LFortran, overlaps completely with the _kind-selector_ _scalar-int-constant-expr_:   letter-spec-lists like `(a)` and `(a-b)` can be parsed either as a letter-spec-list or as an expr. 

With the above grammar, Bison finds a shift/reduce conflict for the INTEGER, REAL, COMPLEX, and LOGICAL intrinsic types that take a _kind-selector_, and the CHARACTER type that takes a _char-selector_.  It eventually resolves it correctly by taking the shift option, but *I* don't know how to express the grammar in a way that prevents this ambiguity.

Using a newer version of Bison, we can get it to produce counterexamples that show the shift/reduce conflict.  This is the output for that diagnostic for a REAL declaration type spec:
```

    "("  shift, and go to state 506

It eventually resolves it correctly by taking the shift option, but *I* don't know how to express the grammar in a way that prevents this ambiguity.

  371                    | KW_REAL • "*" TK_INTEGER

    "*"  shift, and go to state 505
    "("  shift, and go to state 506

    "("       [reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)]
    $default  reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)

    shift/reduce conflict on token "(":
      369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •
      370 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      First example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Shift derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                                            END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: implicit_statement
                  ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                                   sep
                                     ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                            ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec                         "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                   ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                          ↳ 370: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      Second example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Reduce derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                               END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: script_unit
                  ↳ 12: implicit_statement
                        ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                sep
                                           ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                                  ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec      "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                         ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                                ↳ 369: KW_REAL •
```

While these rules handle some simple cases correctly (e.g. `implicit real(8) (a)`), it is easy to generate examples that should be accepted, but are not:
```f90
implicit real(kind=8) (a)
implicit real(4+4) (b)
implicit character(len=20, kind=c_char) (c)
```

Furthering the problem are inputs that are accepted using the `"(" letter_spec_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")"` style of rules, which are never checked or resolved as _scalar-int-constant-expr_:
```f90
program bad_kind
  use constants
  implicit real(r8k) (a)
  implicit real(dp) (b)
  implicit real(q-l) (c)
  print *, kind(a), kind(b), kind(c)
end program bad_kind
```
prints `4    4    4`, without even attempting to lookup values for `r8k` or `dp` or compute the value `q-l`.  Note that these problems are exhibited both before (SHA c75347178f1982e6) and after (SHA aed88c2ec20c7e4591) my commit to add _implicit-spec-list_ 

The appropriate solution to these problems is to change the grammar to simply use the `declaration_type_spec` rule, which handles all of these cases.  That would look like:
```bison
implicit_statement
    : KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE sep { $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE(TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE "(" implicit_none_spec_star ")" sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE2($4, TRIVIA_AFTER($6, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT($2, TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    ;

implicit_spec_list
    : implicit_spec_list "," implicit_spec { $$ = $1; LIST_ADD($$, $3); }
    | implicit_spec { LIST_NEW($$); LIST_ADD($$, $1); }
    ;

implicit_spec
    : declaration_type_spec "(" letter_spec_list ")" {
           $$ = IMPLICIT_SPEC($1, $3, @$); }
    ;
```
The complete implementation of this change can be seen in the draft PR linked below.

The reason that this is draft is that it appears to introduce five *essential* shift/reduce conflicts (see #5426), that don't go away in a standard LALR(1) grammar with reserved keywords.  I believe that this stems from the fact that the `letter_spec` rule, as implemented in LFortran, overlaps completely with the _kind-selector_ _scalar-int-constant-expr_:   letter-spec-lists like `(a)` and `(a-b)` can be parsed either as a letter-spec-list or as an expr. 

With the above grammar, Bison finds a shift/reduce conflict for the INTEGER, REAL, COMPLEX, and LOGICAL intrinsic types that take a _kind-selector_, and the CHARACTER type that takes a _char-selector_.  It eventually resolves it correctly by taking the shift option, but *I* don't know how to express the grammar in a way that prevents this ambiguity.

Using a newer version of Bison, we can get it to produce counterexamples that show the shift/reduce conflict.  This is the output for that diagnostic for a REAL declaration type spec:
```

  371                    | KW_REAL • "*" TK_INTEGER

    "*"  shift, and go to state 505
    "("  shift, and go to state 506

    "("       [reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)]
    $default  reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)

    shift/reduce conflict on token "(":
      369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •
      370 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      First example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Shift derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                                            END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: implicit_statement
                  ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                                   sep
                                     ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                            ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec                         "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                   ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                          ↳ 370: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      Second example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Reduce derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                               END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: script_unit
                  ↳ 12: implicit_statement
                        ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                sep
                                           ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                                  ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec      "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                         ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                                ↳ 369: KW_REAL •

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5621","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5621/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5621/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5621/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5621","id":2725466028,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6ic0-s","number":5621,"title":"Unable to use constants in IMPLICIT statements","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-12-08T19:01:41Z","updated_at":"2024-12-08T19:01:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"During AST->ASR conversion, integer scalar parameters used as kind's in IMPLICIT type declarations are not being found correctly. For example:
```f90
module constants
  integer, parameter :: r8k = 8
end module constants

program typechk
  use constants
  implicit real(r8k)(a)
  print *, kind(a)
end program typechk
```
gives
```
(lf) lfortran % src/bin/lfortran --implicit-typing --show-asr kind.f90
semantic error: Parameter 'r8k' is a variable, which does not reduce to a constant expression
 --> kind.f90:5:1 - 9:19
  |
5 |    program typechk
  |    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^...
...
  |
9 |    end program typechk
  | ...^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 
```
Previous to PR #5618, named constant values were not looked up at all, and the `real(r8k)` would have been emitted as the equivalent of  `real(4)` in the ASR.

PR allows the fixed form parser to parse declarations of the form `<type>*<n> ... function` where `<n>` can have an arbitrary number of digits.  Previously, the number of digits was hard-coded to each type, and would enter an infinite loop if it encountered an unexpected value.

Also adds a test for these declarations.

This allows test `FM715.f` from the FCVS_95 suite to run successfully.

Closes #5350 PR allows the fixed form parser to parse declarations of the form `<type>*<n> ... function` where `<n>` can have an arbitrary number of digits.  Previously, the number of digits was hard-coded to each type, and would enter an infinite loop if it encountered an unexpected value.

Also adds a test for these declarations.

This allows test `FM715.f` from the FCVS_95 suite to run successfully.

Closes #5350 PR allows the fixed form parser to parse declarations of the form `<type>*<n> ... function` where `<n>` can have an arbitrary number of digits.  Previously, the number of digits was hard-coded to each type, and would enter an infinite loop if it encountered an unexpected value.

Also adds a test for these declarations.

This allows test `FM715.f` from the FCVS_95 suite to run successfully.

Closes #5350 `predsci/POT3D`"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-12-10T16:27:49Z","updated_at":"2024-12-10T18:35:53Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"## Description

For the below Fortran program:
```f90
program main
    implicit none
    integer :: x = 1
    call f(x)

    contains

    subroutine f(a)
        integer :: a ! type is declared here for 'a'
        intent(in) :: a ! intent is declared here for 'a'
    end subroutine
end program main

```

probably used in only legacy codebase to define attribute on separate line. The above Fortran program on compilation with LFortran, raises the below error:
```console
> lfortran test.f90
semantic error: Attribute declaration not supported
  --> test.f90:10:9
   |
10 |         intent(in) :: a ! intent is declared here for 'a'
   |         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 


Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
```

while it's compilation with GFortran works without any issue.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5637/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5637/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2532583165","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5637#issuecomment-2532583165","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5637","id":2532583165,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6W9Cb9","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-10T18:35:51Z","updated_at":"2024-12-10T18:35:51Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"This is related to #5355 ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2532583165/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"closed","number":5631,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/pulls/5631","id":2224911346,"node_id":"PR_kwDOCpMuHc6EnXPy","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5631","diff_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5631.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5631.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5631","number":5631,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"Parse more fixed-form function return types","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This PR allows the fixed form parser to parse declarations of the form `<type>*<n> ... function` where `<n>` can have an arbitrary number of digits. Also adds a test for these declarations.

This allows test `FM715.f` from the FCVS_95 suite to run successfully.

Closes #5350 form parser","color":"FE4680","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-11-11T17:21:07Z","updated_at":"2024-12-10T18:45:27Z","closed_at":"2024-12-10T18:45:27Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Trying to parse:
```fortran
      CHARACTER*10 FUNCTION CF716(IVD001)
      END                               
```
LFortran SHA 4068df32ee4 enters an infinite loop in `LCompilers::LFortran::FixedFormRecursiveDescent`. It appears that the `FixedFormRecursiveDescent::is_declaration` function is trying to match against an input string of `0functioncf716(`.  This seems to point to two issues:

1. The `CHARACTER*<N>` parser is not greedy enough getting the repeat count (1 instead of 10?); and
2. There doesn't seem to be a non-match exit criterion from `is_declaration`.

function"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-12-10T23:09:24Z","updated_at":"2024-12-10T23:09:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Trying to compile:
```f90
program test
  print *, amax0(1,2,3)
end program test
```
yields the incorrect error:
```
(lf) lfortran % src/bin/lfortran intrinsics.f90
semantic error: Too many arguments to call `amax0`
 --> intrinsics.f90:2:12
  |
2 |   print *, amax0(1,2,3)
  |            ^^^^^^^^^^^^ 
```

The intrinsic `amax0` should take an arbitrary number of parameters.  The same bug exists in `MAX1`:
```f90
program test
  print *, max1(1.0,2.0,3.0)
end program test
```
```
(lf) lfortran % src/bin/lfortran intrinsics.f90
semantic error: Too many arguments to call `max1`
 --> intrinsics.f90:2:12
  |
2 |   print *, max1(1.0,2.0,3.0)
  |            ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5643/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5643/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

unusual error in `--fixed-form` parsing:
```fortran
      SUBROUTINE SN501
      CHARACTER*2 D2Z1K
      END
```
gives
```
(lf) lfortran % ./src/bin/lfortran  --fixed-form bad.f
syntax error: Token 'z1k' (of type 'identifier') is unexpected here
 --> bad.f:3:21
  |
3 |       CHARACTER*2 D2Z1K
  |                     ^^^ 
```
It strikes me as unusual because this isn't implicit typing nor is `D2` a "reserved" word, as far as I know.

```f90
PROGRAM FM011
  COMMON IACE11(3)
  IACE11(1) = 1
END PROGRAM FM011
```
with
```../lfortran/src/bin/lfortran --implicit-typing  ha.f90          
semantic error: Function 'iace11' not found (not user defined nor intrinsic)
 --> ha.f90:2:10
  |
2 |   COMMON IACE11(3)
  |          ^^^^^^^^^ 
```
The common declaration should be creating the equivalent of `INTEGER :: IACE11(3)`, but lfortran thinks that it is a function.  gfortran compiles correctly.

and, finally if we use the legal alternative syntax for blank common:
```f90
PROGRAM FM011
  COMMON // IACE11(3)
  IACE11(1) = 1
END PROGRAM FM011
```
we get the invalid syntax error 
```
(lf) FCVS_95 % ../lfortran/src/bin/lfortran --implicit-typing  ha.f90          
syntax error: Newline is unexpected here
 --> ha.f90:2:22
  |
2 |   COMMON // IACE11(3)
  |                      ^ 
```
regardless of command-line options.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674","id":2736763681,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6jH7Mh","number":5674,"title":"DISCUSSION: use of more Fortran specifications in AST","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-12-12T19:56:23Z","updated_at":"2024-12-12T19:56:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Caveat: this may already exist and I just haven't found it yet!

Currently, there are many places that accept an `expr` in the parser, and then checks/constraints are enforced on that `expr` tree during AST->ASR conversions. As far as I know, these checks are done in an ad-hoc case-by-case way.

Would it make any sense to add more nodes to the AST that indicate a set of constraints that are expected by the grammar?  For example, the standard defines

_specification-expr_ IS _scalar-int-expr_ IS _int-expr_ IS _expr_

Each of the first three definitions are effectively sets of constraints on the contents of _expr_, but you can't really check those constraints until you've parsed the entire unit and have started building symtabs and the like.  But we do know during parsing that an _explicit-shape-spec_ is expecting one or two _specification-expr_s, so you can still parse a generic `expr`, but wrap it a `specification_expr` AST node and check them consistently in the AST->ASR conversion.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674","id":2736763681,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6jH7Mh","number":5674,"title":"DISCUSSION: use of more Fortran specifications in AST","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-12-12T19:56:23Z","updated_at":"2024-12-12T21:24:47Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Caveat: this may already exist and I just haven't found it yet!

Currently, there are many places that accept an `expr` in the parser, and then checks/constraints are enforced on that `expr` tree during AST->ASR conversions. As far as I know, these checks are done in an ad-hoc case-by-case way.

Would it make any sense to add more nodes to the AST that indicate a set of constraints that are expected by the grammar?  For example, the standard defines

_specification-expr_ IS _scalar-int-expr_ IS _int-expr_ IS _expr_

Each of the first three definitions are effectively sets of constraints on the contents of _expr_, but you can't really check those constraints until you've parsed the entire unit and have started building symtabs and the like.  But we do know during parsing that an _explicit-shape-spec_ is expecting one or two _specification-expr_'s, so you can still parse a generic `expr`, but wrap it a `specification_expr` AST node and check them consistently in the AST->ASR conversion.

I was just using _specification-expr_ as an example, but it seems that there are a lot of places where this technique could be used.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2540042224","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674#issuecomment-2540042224","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5674","id":2540042224,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6XZffw","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-12T21:24:46Z","updated_at":"2024-12-12T21:24:46Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I think it would be in consistency of specification: when we are implementing the parser, we are looking at the standard specification, and know what we need to check.  The AST would then be verifiable documentation of that specification.  The AST->ASR visitors would implement the check once (e.g. an `specification_expr` visitor action), rather than in every place that we we would need to hard-code it in the AST-ASR conversion.  I don't disagree that it may be a *touch* slower, but it is also is much easier to maintain and verify.  

Obviously, we have more pressing things to tackle, but it is just an idea we could consider evolving towards. From 8.5.16 of the F23 draft:
> A variable, common block, or procedure pointer declared in the scoping unit of a main program, module, or
submodule implicitly has the SAVE attribute, which may be confirmed by explicit specification. If a common block
has the SAVE attribute in any other kind of scoping unit, it shall have the SAVE attribute in every scoping unit that is not of a main program, module, or submodule.

This comes into play in 8.10.2.5, "Differences between named common and blank common":
A blank common block has the same properties as a named common block, except for the following.
* Execution of a RETURN or END statement might cause data objects in a named common block to become undefined unless the common block has the SAVE attribute, but never causes nonpointer data objects in blank common to become undefined (19.6.6).
* Named common blocks of the same name shall be of the same size in all scoping units of a program in which they appear, but blank common blocks may be of different sizes.
* A data object in a named common block may be initially defined by means of a DATA statement or type declaration statement in a block data program unit (14.3), but objects in blank common shall not be initially defined.

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2543295513/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5701","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5701/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5701/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5701/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5701","id":2740122630,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6jUvQG","number":5701,"title":"DIMENSION attributes changed by COMMON","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-12-14T20:01:40Z","updated_at":"2024-12-14T20:01:40Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```f90
program main
  implicit none
  integer, parameter :: FLAG1 = 10, FLAG2 = 20
  call set
  call use
  print *, 'okay'
contains
  subroutine set()
    implicit none
    integer flags(2)
    common /cblk/ flags
    flags(1) = FLAG1
    flags(2) = FLAG2
  end subroutine set

  subroutine use()
    implicit none
    integer flags
    common /cblk/ flags(2)
    if (flags(1) /= FLAG1) error stop
    if (flags(2) /= FLAG2) error stop
  end subroutine use
end program main
```
Gives:
```
(lf) lfortran % src/bin/lfortran common.f90
semantic error: Array reference is not allowed on scalar variable
  --> common.f90:20:9
   |
20 |     if (flags(1) /= FLAG1) error stop
   |         ^^^^^^^^ 
```
In a way, this is related to #5355.  According to the F23 standard, section 8.10.2.1, a _common-block-object_ of
> The form _variable-name_ (_array-spec)_ specifies the DIMENSION attribute for that variable.
So, while we need to check for multiple DIMENSION attributes, the common block needs to consist of the variables and attributes accumulated at the end of the _specification-section_:
```f90
  common /ablk/ m, n(2), o
  integer m(2), n, o
  dimension o(2)
```
is correct code that creates a common block of three length-2 integer arrays.

The following program exercises some of these concepts, and runs correctly under gfortran:
```f90
program main
  implicit none
  integer, parameter :: FLAG1 = 10, FLAG2 = 20
  common /ablk/ m, n(2), o
  integer m(2), n, o
  dimension o(2)
  data m / FLAG1, FLAG2/, n /FLAG1, FLAG2/, &
       o / FLAG1, FLAG2/
  call set
  call use
  print *, 'okay'
contains
  subroutine set()
    implicit none
    integer flags(2)
    common /cblk/ flags
    flags(1) = FLAG1
    flags(2) = FLAG2
  end subroutine set

  subroutine use()
    implicit none
    integer flags, m, n, o
    common /cblk/ flags(2)
    common /ablk/ m(2), n(2), o(2)
    save /cblk/
    if (flags(1) /= FLAG1) error stop
    if (flags(2) /= FLAG2) error stop
    if (m(1) /= FLAG1) error stop
    if (m(2) /= FLAG2) error stop
    if (n(1) /= FLAG1) error stop
    if (n(2) /= FLAG2) error stop
    if (o(1) /= FLAG1) error stop
    if (o(2) /= FLAG2) error stop
  end subroutine use
end program main
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5273","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5273/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5273/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5273/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5273","id":2639406530,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6dUiXC","number":5273,"title":"Parser and AST: unify all declarations and statements","user":{"login":"certik","id":20568,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwNTY4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20568?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik","html_url":"https://github.com/certik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6702295011,"node_id":"LA_kwDOCpMuHc8AAAABj3zn4w","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/labels/refactor","name":"refactor","color":"160655","default":false,"description":"Issue or pull request to refactor existing code"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-11-06T22:39:47Z","updated_at":"2024-11-07T15:18:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Right now in the parser we distinguish declarations before "implicit none" and declarations after. In AST we currently store "use" statements separately from the rest of the declarations. Also we separate declarations and body.

At least in the parser itself it seems we should parse all declaration and executable statements together and allow any order. Then
* either when we construct AST we separate them (and report errors if invalid order)
* or possibly we allow any order even in AST, and do the "order" check in the semantic phase in AST->ASR.

This change would allow to report good error messages if the order is incorrect (#5271), as well as allow the "format" statement anywhere (#5272). And it would also simplify the logic both in the parser itself, as well as in constructing the AST. We currently go over all statements already to separate them: https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/blob/fb5bfc447bf71a2515a252c5709feabdcd813c74/src/lfortran/parser/semantics.h#L1512, so the new design should not be slower than what we have now.

In the AST itself we can consider merging `unit_decl1/2` into just `unit_decl`. We can keep use statements, declarations and executable statements separate in AST, but they would all be of type {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5305","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5305/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5305/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5305/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5305","id":2645261714,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6dq32S","number":5305,"title":"Non-standard character-context continuation behavior; do we care?","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-11-08T22:10:19Z","updated_at":"2024-11-08T22:10:19Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"While testing a fix for issue #678, I realized that the `parse_string` in `src/lfortran/parser/parser.cpp` exhibits a variety of non-standard behaviors regarding line continuation in both fixed and free format.  In fixed format, if the parser has not found a single closing quote before the newline, it will simply move to column 7 of the next line, regardless of the state of any of the control columns 1-6, including the lack of a continuation character in column 6.  GCC flags this as an error.

In free format, both LFortran and GCC will compile the program
```f90
program foo
  print  *, "this &
     is a string continuation test"
end program foo
```
by continuing the string to the next non-blank character on the next line ('I').  The standard requires that there is some later continuation line that begins with `&`, and that parsing of the string will continue immediately after that:
 ```f90
program foo
  print  *, "this &
             &is a string continuation test"
end program foo
```

So, the question is, "Do we care?"  I know that a lot of Fortran coders have no idea what the string continuation rules actually are, so I imagine that there is a lot of non-conforming code out there.  At worst, the current implementation accepts some non-conforming code, but it basically "does the right thing", and fixing it is a pretty big pain. 

Thoughts?

  end do
end program test
```
With SHA 1db1cae4e5eae49b1df25dd gives me the warning:

warning: Assuming implicit save attribute for variable declaration

  |
2 |   integer :: calls = 0
  |              ^^^^^^^^^ help: add explicit save attribute or parameter attribute or initialize in a separate statement

```

```

        foo
        []
        []
        ()
        (TriviaNode
            []
            [(EndOfLine)
            (EndOfLine)
            (EndOfLine)]
        )
        []
        []
        []
        [(Declaration
            (AttrType
                TypeInteger
                []
                ()
                ()
                None
            )
            []
            [(calls
            []
            []
            ()
            0
            Equal
            ())]
            ()
        )]
```

```f90
subroutine foo
  integer, save :: calls = 0
  calls = calls + 1
  print *, 'FOO: ', calls
end subroutine foo


program test
  integer i
  do i=1,3
     call foo
  end do
end program test
```
I would expect output similar to GCC:
```sh
 FOO:            1
 FOO:            2
 FOO:            3
```
but I'm getting:
```sh
FOO:     1
FOO:     1
FOO:     1
```
The `AttrSave` attribute does show up in the AST:
```

        foo

            []
            [(EndOfLine)
            (EndOfLine)
            (EndOfLine)]

                TypeInteger
                []
                ()
                ()
                None
            )
            [(SimpleAttribute
                AttrSave
            )]
            [(calls
            []
            []
            ()
            0
            Equal
            ())]
            ()
        )]
        [(Assignment
            0
            calls
            (+ calls 1)
            ()
        )
 ```

{"action":"opened","number":5318,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/pulls/5318","id":2171208353,"node_id":"PR_kwDOCpMuHc6BagKh","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5318","diff_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5318.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5318.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5318","number":5318,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Adjust DATA statement parsing","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Trying to run the FCVS_95 validation suite in #480, I found that some `DATA` statement constructs were not supported in LFortran, such as positive signed integers and complex:
```f90
  data ivon06,ivon07,ivon08,ivon09,ivon10 /+3,+76,+587,+9999,+21111/
  data axva,axvb /(-234.23, 3), (+2, -3.0)/
```
While I was working on fixing those, I noted that the `data_stmt_repeat` grammar rule was too permissive in some cases, and that the `data_stmt_constant` was not supporting _scalar-constant-subobject_, so I tried to fix those as well. {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5355","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5355/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5355/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5355/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5355","id":2650023470,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6d9CYu","number":5355,"title":"Incorrect constraint on attribute specification ordering","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-11-11T18:04:14Z","updated_at":"2024-11-11T18:04:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Attempting to compile:
```f90
subroutine foo
  dimension a(10)
  integer a
end subroutine foo
```
gives the error:
```
semantic error: Cannot set dimension for undeclared variable
 --> func.f90:2:3
  |
2 |   dimension a(10)
  |   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 
```
As annoying as it is, there isn't an enforced ordering between attribute specification statements and type declaration statements. `gfortran@14.2` accepts this.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5356","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5356/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5356/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5356/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5356","id":2650042570,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6d9HDK","number":5356,"title":"Incorrect IMPLICIT typing","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-11-11T18:15:13Z","updated_at":"2024-11-11T18:15:13Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Trying to compile:
```f90
subroutine foo
  implicit integer(l)
  dimension la(10)
  real la
end subroutine foo
```
with `--implicit-typing` gives the error
```
semantic error: Symbol is already declared in the same scope
 --> func.f90:4:8
  |
4 |   real la
  |        ^^ redeclaration
  |
3 |   dimension la(10)
  |             ~~~~~~ original declaration
```
I'm guessing that implicit typing is being applied when `LA` is encountered in the `DIMENSION` attribute, instead of being deferred to the end of the specification section (see also #5355).  This code is accepted by `gfortran@14.2`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5356/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5356/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5341","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5341/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5341/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5341/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5341","id":2649238783,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6d6Cz_","number":5341,"title":"Possibly incorrect way of data initialization","user":{"login":"gxyd","id":7712241,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc3MTIyNDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7712241?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gxyd","html_url":"https://github.com/gxyd","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gxyd/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gxyd/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gxyd/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gxyd/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gxyd/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gxyd/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gxyd/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gxyd/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gxyd/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-11-11T12:45:28Z","updated_at":"2024-11-11T21:32:40Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"## Minimal Reproducible Example

The below fortran program is derived from [tests/fixedform_doloop3.f](https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/blob/main/tests/fixedform_doloop3.f), which I think has an incorrect initialization:
```f90
SUBROUTINE START1()
    IMPLICIT NONE
    INTEGER :: L
    INTEGER :: N
    REAL F, ONE=1, TWO=2
END

program main
end program main

```

the original line in the program `tests/fixedform_doloop3.f` is:
```f90
        REAL F(L), ONE=1, TWO=2
```
here: https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/blob/main/tests/fixedform_doloop3.f#L7

## GFortran output
```console
> gfortran test.f90 -o a.out && ./a.out
test.f90:5:16:

    5 |     REAL F, ONE=1, TWO=2
      |                1
Error: Syntax error in data declaration at (1)
```

## Concern

The concern of this issue *isn't* the **error not reported** bug, but rather the fact that this allows an incorrect way to initialize variables and hence might disturb other correct fixes to the parser. With this issue the main concern is to correct the `tests/fixedform_doloop3.f`, below is a screenshot from https://bayanbox.ir/view/3820206914900746365/StatLab-Applied-Statistics-Algorithms.pdf, where the program `tests/fixedform_doloop3.f` comes from:

We identified at least the following potential speedups:

* [ ] split `asr.h` into smaller parts, and only use what we need; the stock `asr.h` should only contain the ASR definitions that are needed in all of the code base, but specialied visitors, possibly all visitors should be separate
* [ ] modify cmake to not build a library of libasr, but just collect all the files and build everything at once (currently the parallel compilation stalls waiting for the library to be created and linked --- single point bottleneck for some reason in cmake)
* [ ] get rid of some binaries that we have (cpptranslate, various parsing benchmarks), or not build them by default --- it seems link time is very slow.
* [ ] optimize our include header files, and try not to include 3rd party libs at all and minimize C++ stdlib includes (which can also be slow). Only include 3rd party stuff in .cpp files where the damage is limited.
* [ ] switch to ninja
* [ ] moving things from `.h` files to `.cpp` files. We have to be careful and benchmark the speed of compilation (LFortran compiling Fortran code) to ensure there is no regression --- some things have to be inlined. But I suspect most things can be moved to `.cpp` without a penalty.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5414","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5414/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5414/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5414/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5414","id":2670351573,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6fKlTV","number":5414,"title":"Speeding up time to compile LFortran","user":{"login":"certik","id":20568,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwNTY4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20568?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik","html_url":"https://github.com/certik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-11-19T00:00:07Z","updated_at":"2024-11-19T18:17:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Let's speedup the compilation of the C++ source code of LFortran. We identified at least the following potential speedups:

* [ ] split `asr.h` into smaller parts, and only use what we need; the stock `asr.h` should only contain the ASR definitions that are needed in all of the code base, but specialied visitors, possibly all visitors should be separate
* [ ] modify cmake to not build a library of libasr, but just collect all the files and build everything at once (currently the parallel compilation stalls waiting for the library to be created and linked --- single point bottleneck for some reason in cmake)
* [ ] get rid of some binaries that we have (cpptranslate, various parsing benchmarks), or not build them by default --- it seems link time is very slow.
* [ ] optimize our include header files, and try not to include 3rd party libs at all and minimize C++ stdlib includes (which can also be slow). Only include 3rd party stuff in .cpp files where the damage is limited.
* [ ] switch to ninja
* [ ] moving things from `.h` files to `.cpp` files. We have to be careful and benchmark the speed of compilation (LFortran compiling Fortran code) to ensure there is no regression --- some things have to be inlined. But I suspect most things can be moved to `.cpp` without a penalty.

Let's start with those, and then we'll see.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5414/reactions","total_count":5,"+1":5,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5414/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2486429212","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5414#issuecomment-2486429212","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5414","id":2486429212,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6UM-Yc","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-19T18:17:24Z","updated_at":"2024-11-19T18:17:24Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Just a heads up: when running newer versions of Bison (e.g. 3.8.x), the `-r all` option found in `build0.sh` enables counterexample generation, which demonstrates shift/reduce and reduce/reduce conflicts.
